# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-governance/glossary/assets\#how-to-add-assets-to-glossary-terms)  How to Add Assets to Glossary Terms

After creating a glossary term, data assets can be associated with the term. In the **Glossary Term > Assets Tab** all the assets associated with the glossary term are displayed. These data assets are further subgrouped as Tables, Topics, Dashboards, etc.![Assets Tab](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/7tzzR5Et0bE_K92r/public/images/how-to-guides/governance/term3.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=7tzzR5Et0bE_K92r&q=85&s=f93a5d4d514d1de68c61e10ca01bdc1a)You can add more assets by clicking on **Add > Assets**.![Add Asset](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/UFTj-NKqttMXADX2/public/images/how-to-guides/governance/asset.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=UFTj-NKqttMXADX2&q=85&s=db94bc143e4dc861d9174d18ad5f1b11)You can further search and filter assets by type. Simply select the relevant assets and click **Save**.![Assets Related to the Glossary Term](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/UFTj-NKqttMXADX2/public/images/how-to-guides/governance/asset1.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=UFTj-NKqttMXADX2&q=85&s=cd6c0b81c0ba6e8eec0fc23c575573b4)The glossary term lists the Assets, which makes it easy to discover all the data assets related to the term.

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-governance/glossary/assets\#glossary-terms-and-tags)  Glossary Terms and Tags

If **Tags** are associated with a **Glossary Term**, then applying that glossary term to a data asset, will also automatically apply the associated tags to that data asset. For example, the glossary term ‘Account’ has a PII.Sensitive tag associated with it. When you add a glossary term to a data asset, the associated tags also get added.![Glossary Term and Associated Tags](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/x0xXkTWAgiDpM5Ry/public/images/how-to-guides/governance/tag5.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=x0xXkTWAgiDpM5Ry&q=85&s=d45cc16723b75d5aec14cf355293afbe)![Glossary Term and Tag gets Added to the Data Asset](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/x0xXkTWAgiDpM5Ry/public/images/how-to-guides/governance/tag6.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=x0xXkTWAgiDpM5Ry&q=85&s=e489426627cf97b4492acc165a03bd2b)

Here are the Top 8 Best Practices around Terminologies.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-governance/glossary/best-practices)

- [🔍 Evaluation Result](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger#-evaluation-result)
- [📊 Evaluation Summary](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger#-evaluation-summary)
- [Use Cases](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger#use-cases)

# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#permission-debugger)  Permission Debugger

The **Permission Debugger** allows administrators to analyze and debug user permissions across roles, teams, and policies. This feature is critical for troubleshooting access issues, verifying policy behavior, and ensuring users have the correct level of access.

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#how-it-works)  How It Works

The Permission Debugger simulates permission checks for a given user on a selected resource and operation. It provides detailed insight into:

- Which policies and rules were evaluated
- The final allow/deny decision
- Matching rule count
- Evaluation time

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#how-to-use-the-permission-debugger)  How to Use the Permission Debugger

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#1-select-a-user)  1\. Select a User

First navigate to settings > Access Control > Permission Debugger
Use the input field to search for and select the user whose permissions you want to inspect.![Select a User in Permission Debugger](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger/permission1.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=ef1beeec445c507c9aa34aca053fd965)

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#2-define-the-permission-check)  2\. Define the Permission Check

- **Resource**: Select a resource type.  - Options include: `user`, `team`, `table`, `database`, `glossary`, `tag`, `glossaryTerm`, `searchIndex`, `mlModel`, `container`, `topic`, `pipeline`, `dashboard`, `databaseSchema`
- **Operation**: Choose the operation to check against the selected resource.  - Examples: `ViewAll`, `EditAll`, `Deploy`, `Trigger`, `Kill`, `GenerateToken`, etc.
- **Resource FQN or ID (Optional)**: Provide a Fully Qualified Name (FQN) or unique ID of a specific resource if you want to debug at the resource instance level.

![Define the Permission Check](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger/permission2.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=c35f21c97b9a8bd718415d3b056463b4)

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#3-evaluate-permission)  3\. **Evaluate** Permission

Click the **Evaluate** button to perform the permission check.![Evaluate Permission](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger/permission3.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=6a4090d986a62c0242be0f21a1908f19)

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#example-1-denied-editall-on-table)  Example 1: DENIED (EditAll on Table)

![Permission Debugger Example: DENIED](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger/permission4.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=9aa82810350ecb2379c6b2b229f487a1)**User**: `prajwal.pp44` **Resource**: `table` **Operation**: `EditAll` **Resource FQN**: `sample_data.ecommerce_db.shopify.dim_address_clean`**Result**:

> **Decision: DENIED**
> User `prajwal.pp44` is **Denied** to perform `EditAll` on
> `table (sample_data.ecommerce_db.shopify.dim_address_clean)`

**Evaluation Summary**:

| Metric | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Policies Evaluated | 2 |
| Rules Evaluated | 1048 |
| Matching Rules | 0 |
| Allow Rules | 0 |
| Deny Rules | 0 |
| Time | 354ms |

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#example-2-allowed-viewall-on-table)  Example 2: ALLOWED (ViewAll on Table)

![Permission Debugger Example: ALLOWED](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger/permission5.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=2ba7065b313b2509925a2d4be74c3734)This example demonstrates a **successful permission evaluation** for a user attempting to view a specific table resource using the `ViewAll` operation.

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#-scenario)  📄 Scenario

- **User**: `prajwal.pp44`
- **Resource**: `table`
- **Operation**: `ViewAll`
- **Resource FQN**: `sample_data.ecommerce_db.shopify.dim_address_clean`

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#-evaluation-result)  🔍 Evaluation Result

> **Decision: ALLOWED**
> User `prajwal.pp44` is **Allowed** to perform `ViewAll` on
> `table (sample_data.ecommerce_db.shopify.dim_address_clean)`

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#-evaluation-summary)  📊 Evaluation Summary

| Detail | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Policies Evaluated** | 2 |
| **Rules Evaluated** | 1048 |
| **Matching Rules** | 1046 |
| **Allow Rules** | 0 |
| **Deny Rules** | 0 |
| **Evaluation Time** | 363ms |

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/permission-debugger\#use-cases)  Use Cases

- Debug permission issues for a specific user.
- Validate that newly created policies are functioning as expected.
- Understand why a user has or doesn’t have access to specific resources.

# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#overview-of-mcp)  Overview of MCP

OpenMetadata’s MCP Server gives technical or non-technical users the ability to interact with your organization’s metadata through natural language conversations via systems such as ChatGPT or Claude.

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#what-is-mcp)  What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an emerging open standard (spearheaded by Anthropic and embraced by many industry leaders) that helps AI systems interact with external tools and data in a uniform, secure way. MCP works as a “universal translator” between AI assistants (or any LLM-driven application) and the myriad of systems where data and knowledge reside. Instead of building one-off integrations or brittle scripts for each data source, MCP provides a common interface.In technical terms, MCP lets systems expose their capabilities - the data they hold and the actions they can perform - in a machine-readable schema that AI models can understand. For example, through MCP a data platform could advertise tools (functions an AI can call, like lookup\_customer\_by\_email), resources (datasets or knowledge bases an AI can query), or even prompt templates that guide interactions. An AI assistant connected via MCP can then securely retrieve information or trigger actions by invoking these standardized functions, with proper authorization.For organizations, MCP promises to bridge the gap between powerful AI reasoning and real-world data context. With a single, consistent protocol, an AI assistant can maintain awareness of business-specific context as it moves between different tools and datasets. Just as HTTP standardized how clients talk to servers, MCP is standardizing how AI models connect with data sources. It’s a simpler, more scalable way to give AI access to the knowledge it needs to produce relevant, accurate results.

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#adding-an-mcp-server-to-openmetadata)  Adding an MCP Server to OpenMetadata

Even the best LLMs need context in order to operate effectively. We added an MCP Server to OpenMetadata to unlock a new level of value from its unified knowledge graph. By embedding an MCP server directly into OpenMetadata, the platform can now expose rich metadata context to AI assistants and other MCP clients in real time. This means an AI tool like ChatGPT or Claude can query OpenMetadata to ask such questions as, “What is the definition of this metric?”, “Show me the lineage of data feeding this dashboard”, or “Who is the owner of this dataset and when was it last updated?” - and get answers based on live organizational metadata.All the relationships and context captured in OpenMetadata’s graph become available to augment AI-driven analyses and automations. What makes this particularly powerful is that OpenMetadata’s implementation of MCP is enterprise-ready by design.

Embedding an MCP Server into OpenMetadata - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[Embedding an MCP Server into OpenMetadata](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AuYBaXC8-M4) [OpenMetadata](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCASsxvcVlbxzT-nd2Vh2ocg)

OpenMetadata47.1K subscribers

[Watch on](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AuYBaXC8-M4)

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#installing-mcp-server)  Installing MCP Server

MCP is an OpenMetadata application that is installed by default, if it is not downloaded:

- Go to `<YOUR-OpenMetadata-SERVER>/marketplace/apps/McpApplication` and select _Install_

![Add MCP app](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA/public/images/how-to-guides/mcp/install-mcp.jpg?fit=max&auto=format&n=AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA&q=85&s=950e2715f0774fcdbecd32b0aecc6fc9)

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#authentication)  Authentication

OpenMetadata’s MCP Server supports two authentication methods:

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#oauth-2-0-recommended)  OAuth 2.0 (Recommended)

Connect using your existing OpenMetadata login - the same way you sign in to the OpenMetadata UI. No tokens to generate or manage. OAuth works with all supported SSO providers (Google, Azure, Okta, Auth0, LDAP, SAML, and more).Learn more about [OAuth 2.0 Authentication](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/oauth).

### [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#personal-access-token-pat)  Personal Access Token (PAT)

For environments where browser-based login is not available, you can use a Personal Access Token.

- Go to `<YOUR-OpenMetadata-SERVER>/users/<YOUR-USERNAME>/access-token` and select _Generate New Token_. This will give your AI agent the same role and access policy that is assigned to you in OpenMetadata. If you would like it to have different role-based access controls, create a new user.

![Generate New Token](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA/public/images/how-to-guides/mcp/generate-new-token.jpg?fit=max&auto=format&n=AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA&q=85&s=71d65a340269f12a588dd2bfaa2ba5f3)

- Set your _Token Expiration_. Once your new token is created copy it.

![Set Token Lifespan](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA/public/images/how-to-guides/mcp/generate-new-token-2.jpg?fit=max&auto=format&n=AdlqT7tGpGl0YynA&q=85&s=605cc9098b0807a7b7d76ef886857154)

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp\#connect-your-mcp-client)  Connect Your MCP Client

With MCP installed, connect your preferred AI assistant to start prompting with OpenMetadata:

[**OAuth 2.0 Authentication** \\
\\
Secure, token-free authentication using your existing SSO login.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/oauth)

[**MCP Server Connection Guide** \\
\\
Connect to your MCP Server.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/connect)

[**MCP Tools Reference** \\
\\
Detailed examples and usage patterns for all available OpenMetadata MCP tools.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/reference)

[**Getting Started with Claude Desktop** \\
\\
Connect OpenMetadata to Anthropic’s popular AI assistant.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/claude)

[**Getting Started with Cursor** \\
\\
Connect OpenMetadata to Cursor IDE.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/cursor)

[**Getting Started with VS Code** \\
\\
Connect OpenMetadata to Visual Studio Code.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/vscode)

[**Getting Started with Claude Code** \\
\\
Connect OpenMetadata to Claude Code CLI.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/claude-code)

[**Getting Started with Goose** \\
\\
Connect OpenMetadata to Block’s open-source AI assistant.](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/mcp/goose)

# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/guide-for-data-users/browser-ext\#openmetadata-browser-extension)  OpenMetadata Browser Extension

OpenMetadata’s Browser Extension bridges the gap between users and metadata. Visit the Chrome webstore and search for OpenMetadata to add the extension to your Chrome browser with just a few clicks. You can find the Chrome browser extension [here](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/openmetadata/ndjnpiadedlmgddlpeklbnobebkpkdgb)Once installed, pin the extension for easy accessibility. Simply click on the OpenMetadata icon in your Chrome browser to start using the extension. Add the base URL for your OpenMetadata instance, and connect by signing in. Once signed in, the OpenMetadata Chrome extension will display your Activity Feeds from My Data page. While working on a third party tool, which is integrated with OpenMetadata, you can simply click on the extension to view the associated metadata to access details about ownership, description, associated tags, glossary terms, schema, lineage, custom properties, and more.Watch the video to learn how you can access all the metadata you need, directly into your active workspace.

Collate Chrome Browser Extension (v1.11) - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[Collate Chrome Browser Extension (v1.11)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3xdwd_vEQQU) [Collate](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCZSvE7AOCvOTEGiANrXAIUg)

Collate43.2K subscribers

[Watch on](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3xdwd_vEQQU)

If you are using your own OpenMetadata instance which utilises Single Sign-On (SSO), you need to add the following URL to your SSO redirect list:`https://ndjnpiadedlmgddlpeklbnobebkpkdgb.chromiumapp.org/auth0`

For more information on how to add URL to SSO redirect list, you can visit this [page](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/deployment/security).

# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-landing-page-customization/defining-persona\#how-to-define-personas)  How to Define Personas

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-landing-page-customization/defining-persona\#what-is-a-persona)  What is a Persona?

In OpenMetadata, a **persona** represents an organization’s specific role or function, such as a **data engineer**, **data steward**, or **data scientist**. Personas define how the user interacts with the platform by tailoring the interface and functionality to their role.**Example**:

- A **data engineer** might focus on pipelines and data quality.
- A **data steward** is more concerned with governance and glossary management.

## [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-landing-page-customization/defining-persona\#steps-to-define-a-persona)  Steps to Define a Persona:

1. As an administrator, navigate to **Settings > Personas** in OpenMetadata.

![Settings > Personas](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-1.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=2ea46c13b70fa5f4103cf4d127ccaa83)

2. **Create a new persona** or modify an existing one.

![create a new persona*](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-3.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=1a616d13262024502457e625291490e7)

3. **Associate users** with the appropriate persona to customize their experience based on their roles.

![Associate users](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/persona-4.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=ed15d12fdb9061d9d9350d6b6cc449dc)

# [​](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/admin-guide/delete-service-connection\#how-to-delete-a-service-connection)  How to Delete a Service Connection

To delete a service connection, navigate to the service page and click on the ⋮ icon on the right of the page, and click on **Delete**.![Delete a Service Connection](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/delete1.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=3d085214682472eda10d839ba571d921)To permanently delete the database, type DELETE and Confirm.![Permanently Delete the Database](https://mintcdn.com/openmetadata/qho-E1y9MslmzIeO/public/images/how-to-guides/admin-guide/delete2.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=qho-E1y9MslmzIeO&q=85&s=2bd575868f17b252534f60b962c17a56)
